title: The Distribution of Nonwage Benefits: Maternity Benefits and Gender Diversity
abstract: Why do firms offer nonwage compensation instead of the equivalent amount in financial compensation? We argue that firms use nonwage benefits, specifically female-friendly benefits, such as maternity leave, to increase gender diversity by efficiently attracting women. Using Glassdoor data, we show that firms offer higher-quality maternity leave benefits in labor markets in which female talent is relatively scarce. This result also holds more generally when examining all female-friendly nonwage benefits and is not present when looking at gender-neutral benefits. Moreover, using staggered adoption of state laws, we show that voluntary provision of these benefits can increase firm value.

author.Tim Liu*: David Eccles School of Business,University of Utah, USA
author.Paige Ouimet: Kenan-Flagler Business School, UNC Chapel Hill, USA
author.Elena Simintzi: Kenan-Flagler Business School, UNC Chapel Hill and CEPR, USA

article.1. Nonwage Benefit Data and Sample Const:
JEL G30 - General J30 - General J32 - Nonwage Labor Costs and Benefits; Retirement Plans; Private Pensions Issue Section: Articles

Nonwage benefits—any form of compensation or perk offered to employees in addition to their monetary compensation—represent 30%of total employee compensation, on average (Bureau of Labor Statistics). Most nonwage benefits are voluntarily provided by the firm, costly to provide, and not universally valued by all employees. Moreover, there is significant heterogeneity in the distribution of nonwage benefits across firms, begging the question: what drives the distribution of these benefits?

In this paper, we propose a sorting explanation wherein firms use benefits that are disproportionately valuable to employees with certain characteristics to target those employees who are more valuable to the firm but who are relatively more difficult to hire and retain otherwise (Lazear 1986,1998). To test this hypothesis, we focus on firms’ use of maternity leave, a type of nonwage benefit that is especially valuable to women, as a means to increase gender diversity.

Achieving a gender-balanced workforce has become increasingly important for firms. This goal of gender balance may be driven by economic arguments, for example, that a diverse employment profile can facilitate the consideration of a more comprehensive set of solutions and strategies, thereby producing higher-quality decisions (Hillman, Shropshire, and Cannella 2007;Dezso and Ross 2012;Matsa and Miller 2013;Flabbi et al. 2019). Alternatively, firms may take actions to increase gender equality because they believe it is the right thing to do or they want to avoid unfavorable media exposure. Or firms may prioritize diversity in response to pressure from policy makers or investors (Ahern and Dittmar 2012;Gormley et al. 2021).

The goal of a gender-diverse workforce is more challenging in sectors in which female talent is underrepresented. We argue that when qualified female employees are in limited supply, firms offer relatively more generous maternity leave benefits to attract and retain female talent. In an example from a male-dominated industry, in 2007 Google extended its maternity leave in an effort to improve retention of female talent. An internal study found that after extending paid maternity leave to 18 weeks from 12 weeks, “returning mothers left Google at half the rate they were previously” (Steel 2015).

Maternity leave benefits offer a clean setting to test sorting as an explanation for why firms offer nonwage benefits. First, maternity benefits are clearly important to women. Women disproportionately take advantage of parental leave relative to men, even within firms that offer similar benefits to both. For example, using data on claims from California’s paid family leave program,Bana, Bedard, and Rossin-Slater (2018)find that new mothers are 3.92 times more likely to make a bonding claim for parental leave, compared with new fathers. As such, firms providing more generous maternity leave may be able to increase diversity by recruiting and retaining more female employees, who expect to utilize these benefits or who view them as a signal of a female-friendly workplace.

Second, that paid maternity leave is not currently legally mandated at the federal level in the United States offers firms a unique opportunity to differentiate themselves from others by providing this benefit as part of their compensation package. While the U.S. labor law the Family and Medical Leave Act of 1993 (FMLA) provides up to 12 weeks of unpaid leave that can be used to care for a newborn child, unpaid leave has been shown to have little to no impact on female employment and wages (Blau and Kahn 2017). Moreover, offering maternity leave benefits has an additional advantage in that it allows firms to increase diversity without paying unequal wages across genders, an action that could potentially create an impression of unfairness (Akerlof and Yellen 1990) or possibly expose the firm to litigation risk.

To test our hypothesis, we draw on a novel data set provided by Glassdoor, which comprehensively covers the different types of nonwage benefits offered by firms, including maternity leave benefits. A unique feature of this data set is its employee rankings of benefits on a 1- to 5-point scale, representing a measure of the benefit quality. Moreover, we observe information on worker characteristics, including gender, education, age, wages, industry, and location of their employment.

Attracting qualified female employees is tougher in labor markets with a lower supply of female talent. Even though 50.4%of college-educated workers are female, the heterogeneity across industries is significant. We predict that firms facing supply constraints will be more likely to use maternity leave benefits to increase diversity. We measure the labor supply of female talent as the percentage of female employees with a college degree at the industry level. Consistent with our intuition, we find a correlation between firms in industries with relatively more high-skilled female workers and lower maternity benefit ratings: a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female college-educated employees in an industry is associated with a decrease in expected maternity benefits by 0.10 rating points, reflecting 0.08 of the standard deviation in maternity benefit ratings.

In our empirical analysis, we control for individual characteristics, including individuals’ gender, education, age, wages, and whether they are past or current employees of the firm they are rating, to ensure that our results are not driven by respondent characteristics. We also control for firm-level characteristics to capture the fact that firms that have greater scale, better profitability, less debt, and more investment opportunities may offer higher-quality maternity leave benefits. Our specifications include year fixed effects to absorb macroeconomic shocks and state fixed effects to account for potential correlation between firm spatial characteristics and the generosity of firm compensation packages.

We generalize the sorting mechanism, wherein firms offer relatively more generous benefits that are disproportionately attractive to female employees when female talent is scarce, beyond maternity benefits. To this end, we expand our sample to include all available ratings of nonwage benefits in Glassdoor and classify each benefit into one of two groups: female-friendly benefits and gender-neutral benefits. Our results continue to hold when we consider all female-friendly benefits. These results are economically important: a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female college-educated employees in an industry is associated with a decrease in expected female-friendly benefits by 0.10 rating points, reflecting 0.08 of the standard deviation in female-friendly benefit ratings.

This analysis also can help address potential concerns that omitted variables might be driving the relation between the supply of female talent and the quality of maternity leave benefits (or female-friendly benefits more generally). For example, firms in industries with a low supply of female talent could offer more generous compensation packages overall, not just better maternity leave benefits. To the extent that overall compensation generosity drives the correlation between maternity benefits and female talent, we should also find a significant correlation between the female talent scarcity and gender-neutral benefits offered by the firm. Instead, we find that the supply of female talent does not significantly correlate with gender-neutral benefits.

To further address the potential for remaining unobserved firm or user characteristics, we include both gender-neutral and female-friendly benefits in a pooled regression specification that allows us to control for granular fixed effects. We include benefit fixed effects to account for systematic differences in ratings across different benefits, firm-year fixed effects to absorb any time-varying firm-specific characteristics that could correlate with the generosity of firms’ compensation packages, and user-firm-year fixed effects to account for any individual-level differences in ratings generosity for a given firm at a given point in time. We show that, conditional on a given user-firm-year, firms offer relatively more generous female-friendly benefits, compared with gender-neutral benefits, in industries in which female talent is limited.

We further perform additional robustness analysis of our baseline finding that maternity benefit quality increases with scarcity of female talent. We show our results are robust to instead measuring the share of female college workers at the state level. A one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female high-skilled employees in a state is associated with a decrease in expected maternity benefits by 0.05 rating points, reflecting 0.04 of the standard deviation in maternity benefit ratings. One advantage of relying on this geographic specification is that it allows us to implement an instrumental variables specification. In this case, we instrument for the share of college-educated females in a given state using geographic variation in men’s mobilization rates during World War II (WWII). Our identifying assumption is that geographic variation in WWII mobilization of males generated a persistent effect on the share of college-educated female workers and did not directly affect future decisions by local firms to provide generous maternity benefits. Our results are robust to this instrumental variables estimation.

The measure of maternity benefit ratings captures both quantitative features, such as weeks of paid leave, and qualitative features, such as whether the firm’s culture encourages employees to use these benefits. Given differences in norms and the implementation of nonwage benefits across firms, the quality of maternity benefits can be more informative than their presence. For example, the policy of offering a certain number of weeks of paid maternity leave may not be especially informative by itself since women entitled to maternity benefits often choose to opt out if they think they will be valued less at work (Zagorsky 2017). Nevertheless, we validate our baseline results using crowdsourced data on paid weeks of maternity leave. These results are measured at the firm, rather than the individual, level and are thus not subject to potential biases driven by either individual perceptions (rather than the true quality of the rating) or sample selection concerns associated with the type of employees who provide ratings on Glassdoor. Our results are robust to this alternative measure.

The key implication of the sorting hypothesis we examine is that by offering better quality maternity benefits when female talent is scarce, firms can achieve a relatively more gender-diverse workforce. We provide evidence consistent with this argument. First, we show suggestive evidence that job seekers are likely aware of these benefits, as firms that offer higher-quality maternity benefits tend to mention these benefits in their online job ads. Second, we collect data on the share of female employees at the firm level and find that firms that offer higher-quality maternity benefits, while operating in industries with scarce female talent, employ a relatively larger share of female employees.

Third, we argue this is efficient by showing that offering maternity benefits to attract and retain female talent that is in scarce supply has implications for firm value. To this end, we use plausibly exogenous variation in the staggered introduction or expansion of paid family leave legislation in seven states and the District of Columbia. These laws created programs, financed by taxes, that provide a minimum level of paid family leave for employees within the state, thereby reducing the relative attractiveness of maternity leave plans already offered by firms voluntarily. If firmsvoluntarilyoffer these plans to gain a competitive advantage in terms of increasing diversity, and this creates value, then an increase in maternity benefits by their peers should result in lower value at these firms. We show results consistent with this argument. Firms that offered more generous maternity benefits prior to the legislation, while operating in labor markets in which female talent is scarce, lose value upon announcement of these laws, relative to firms in other states with no such legislation. These results are consistent with maternity benefits adding value for firms that voluntarily adopt them.

Our paper contributes to the literature that studies the drivers of nonwage benefits. Firms may be able to provide nonwage benefits at a lower cost, relative to the employee purchasing the same benefits for self-consumption. Such discounts may arise because of bulk purchasing discounts (Woodbury 1983). Moreover, several nonwage benefits are subject to preferential tax treatment, which similarly reduces the cost of offering such benefits instead of the equivalent dollar amount in wages (Feldstein 1973;Cymrot 1980).Oyer (2008)finds empirical support for these arguments, showing, in addition, that employers offer nonwage benefits to mitigate the disutility of work for groups of employees with relatively longer work hours. Our paper extends this earlier literature, examining sorting as a new mechanism that can explain why firms choose to compensate their employees with nonwage benefits. We also improve the measurement of nonwage benefits in two fundamental ways. First, we can observe the individual’s firm, which allows us to merge financial firm characteristics to control for cross-sectional differences in firms’ compensation policies and even examine within-firm differences in some of our analysis. Second, our information on the relative quality of benefits allows us to measure them more precisely. Taking advantage of our data, in Section1.1, we present novel summary statistics using broad categories of nonwage benefits offered by our sample firms.

We also build on the literature showing that compensation policies are important for attracting employees. This literature has focused primarily on using equity-based pay to attract more optimistic and productive workers, as inLazear (2000),Oyer and Schaefer (2005), andBergman and Jenter (2007), or workers who have greater trust in the firm’s management, as inTsui and Vance (2020). These earlier papers explore sorting on nonobservable characteristics. We contribute to this literature by showing that sorting on an observable characteristic, gender, can also help to explain why firms offer nonwage benefits.

Finally, our paper studies the role of firm policies in attracting a (gender) diverse workforce.Tate and Yang (2015)show that female leadership cultivates more female-friendly culture inside the firm.Bennedsen et al. (2022)show that laws mandating disclosure of disaggregated wage statistics by gender lead to a reduction of the gender pay gap and to greater female hiring rates. We instead argue that noncash compensation, in the form of maternity leave benefits, increases firms’ gender diversity and has positive value implications. Increasing diversity using nonmonetary compensation might be more efficient than paying higher wages to women, given that relative pay concerns among employees have been shown to have negative effects on productivity (Breza, Kaur, and Shamdasani 2018). Our paper is complementary toBennett et al. (2021), who find that mandated paid family leave laws are associated with higher productivity in affected firms. Our paper differs in that we focus on the voluntary provision of benefits and instead use state-level mandates as a source of exogenous variation in the relative attractiveness of voluntarily provided benefits.
1.1 An overview of nonwage benefits

We use data from Glassdoor, a large crowdsourcing company that provides data on wages as well as nonwage benefits. Information is reported for 54 separate nonwage benefits, such as health insurance, 401K plans, and vacation and paid time off. Respondents on the Glassdoor website are asked to report their ratings of nonwage benefits on a 1- to 5-point ranking scale. Employees are also asked to report details on their current employment, occupation, and personal characteristics, such as gender, education, age, and employment location. Our access to confidential micro-data provided to us by Glassdoor allows us to observe detailed information for the employees providing the ratings. For example, whereas the publicly available version on its website reports only the mean and range of salaries for a given occupation and firm, we observe granular salary information for individuals in a given occupation and firm. The website also reports information on benefit ratings but does not allow for these ratings to be linked to employee characteristics.

Despite the importance of nonwage compensation, our understanding of the distribution of nonwage benefits at firms is limited. Prompted by this scarcity of evidence, we first present descriptive statistics using broad nonwage benefits categories we created based on our Glassdoor data. For ease of exposition, we have grouped the 54 unique benefit categories from Glassdoor into 8 broad benefit groups: Health, Life and Casualty Insurance, Retirement, Equity-Based Compensation, Training and Education, Leave and Vacation, Flexibility, and Perks. To facilitate comparison with our baseline analysis to follow, we keep maternity benefits—the focus of our paper—as a stand-alone category.

Table 1displays univariate correlations between benefit ratings and firm characteristics. Dark (light) shaded cells represent correlations that are significant at the 1%(5%⁠) level. We find that firm size (ln(Assets)) tends to be positively correlated with benefit quality. This is consistent with the argument that firms can act as a buyer’s club for its employees, spreading out the fixed costs associated with benefit provision or receiving bulk discounts on benefits (Oyer 2008). We find that the correlation between profitability (Profit margin) and benefit quality tends to be positive, although this is significant for only one of the benefit categories. Similarly, we document a consistently negative correlation between benefit quality and leverage (Debt/Assets). These results suggest that benefit quality is a function of financial slack, similar to results found inMichaels, Page, and Whited (2019)looking at wages and financial constraints. We find generally positive and often significant correlations between investment opportunities (q) and benefit quality. Interestingly, the benefit group with the highest correlation withqis Equity-Based Compensation, which includes benefits commonly associated with the high-growth technology sector.Ittner, Lambert, and Larcker (2003)find that these firms place particular importance on employee retention, relative to firms in more established industries, potentially explaining this correlation.

We also find that firm-level average base pay (ln(Base pay)) is positively correlated with benefit quality across the majority of benefit groups. It is intuitive that workers who receive higher cash wages also receive higher noncash benefits, on average. Our subsequent analysis mostly focuses on maternity leave, a specific nonwage benefit, though maternity leave interestingly behaves similarly to other benefit categories when it comes to correlations with firm characteristics.

Figure 1displays average benefit ratings by benefit group and across time for the sample of public firms. We find that Flexibility, Retirement, and Maternity are the three highest-rated benefits. On the other hand, Life and Casualty Insurance, Equity-Based Compensation, and Training and Education appear to be the three lowest-rated benefits. Across all benefit groups, we observe a time trend whereby benefit ratings across the second half of our sample (2017 –2019) is higher than the first half (2014–2016). This trend is consistent with the increasing share of total compensation that nonwage compensation composes; greater spending on nonwage compensation over time translates into improved benefit quality. Moreover, we document the largest increase in average maternity benefit ratings.

Figure 2presents summary statistics of benefits by industry.Manufacturing,Professional services, andAgriculture, natural resources, utilities, and constructionsectors exhibit high-quality benefits throughout our sample period. Given the high rates of unionization in both utilities and manufacturing, these findings are consistent with arguments inFreeman and Medoff (1984)that predict unionized employees will have more generous benefit packages. The high ratings inProfessional servicesare consistent with human capital intensive industries offering high compensation packages to draw on talented employees. Moreover, across most industries, we observe the same positive time trend whereby average benefit ratings increase in the second half of our sample (2017–2019). The biggest increase in benefit quality took place in theHealth and educationsector.

1.2 Maternity benefits Glassdoor sample

Having documented important trends in benefits and cross-sectional variation across time, we now turn our attention to the main analysis of the paper where we test whether a sorting hypothesis can explain why firms offer nonwage benefits with a focus on maternity benefits. Critical to the analysis that follows is a specific benefit in Glassdoor titled “Maternity & paternity leave.” While this variable measures parental leave more broadly, evidence suggests that these benefits are disproportionately offered to and used by women. As such, we refer to this benefit simply as “maternity” leave hereinafter for parsimony. For example, using data from the Great Places to Work,Goldin, Kerr, and Olivetti (2020)find that the average firm offers approximately five fewer weeks of paid leave to fathers, relative to mothers. Second, even when men and women are offered identical benefits, women utilize the benefits more. Using data on paid family leave claims from California,Bana, Bedard, and Rossin-Slater (2018)find that new mothers are nearly four times more likely to make a bonding claim relative to new fathers. Moreover, offering better quality maternity benefits can signal a female-friendly culture.

Our sample of nonwage benefits covers the period from July 2, 2014, through April 25, 2019. To form our baseline sample of maternity leave benefit ratings, we require that all individuals in the sample are regular employees of the firm. We drop part-time, contractor, and other nonregular employees, for whom benefits may not be available. In addition, we require that individuals in the sample provide information on demographic characteristics and whether they rate their current or past job. In addition, since our main analysis in the paper requires measuring the supply of female talent at the industry level, we identify each firm’s four-digit NAICS industry designation from Compustat. Our baseline sample consists of 3,157 unique review-level observations representing 1,094 unique publicly listed firms. The average firm has 2.2 reviews of maternity benefits per firm-year. Conditioning the analysis on public firms has the added benefit in that it allows us to control for firm-level characteristics that are important determinants of firms’ compensation policies. TableA.1in the appendix presents the detailed steps taken to construct our baseline sample.

Table 2presents summary statistics for key variables used in our baseline sample of public firms. The average maternity benefit rating is 3.86 (of 5), and the median is 4. Survey respondents have the option to review their current or a previous job. We find that 68%of the reviews refer to a current job and 32%to a previous employment. Of the respondents who rate maternity leave benefits, 46%are female and 89%have at least a college degree. In comparison, 48%of people employed in the United States are female and 36%of those employed have a college degree, according to nationally representative data collected by the American Community Survey (ACS), Census Bureau. This difference (shown inInternet Appendix A Figures IA1andIA2, respectively) suggests that our sample is biased toward high-skilled workers. Our sample of employees is also younger, compared to the distribution of working age Americans, with the average respondent in our sample being 36.8 years old. In fact, 67.7%of the employees in our Glassdoor sample are under 40 years of age (versus 41.4%in the U.S. Census). Workers over 50 years of age are underrepresented, reflecting only 11%of our sample (versus 38.1%in the U.S. Census) (Internet Appendix A Figure IA3). On average, individuals in our sample have an annual base pay of $86,640, which corresponds to the 79th percentile of income in the United States, consistent with the fact that our sample overrepresents high-skilled workers. The fact that our sample is biased toward more educated, younger, and higher-paid workers does not seem, however, to affect our results.1

The firms in our sample tend to be larger compared to the typical Compustat firm. As shown inTable 2, the average (median) firm in our sample has $205 ($24.2) billion in assets, while the average (median) firm in Compustat has $7.7 ($0.4) billion. The average (median) firm has a profit margin of 0.09 (0.09), compared to−2.72 (0.04) for the average (median) firm in Compustat. Our sample firms have an average (median) leverage ratio and Tobin’s q of 23%(20%⁠) and 3.23 (2.17), respectively. In comparison, the typical Compustat firm has an average (median) leverage ratio and Tobin’s q of 43%(19%⁠) and 2.30 (1.41), respectively. Our sample covers the largest and most important firms in the U.S. economy; however, this raises the concern that our results may not be generalizable to the smallest firms. This external validity concern is alleviated in our analysis using states or Glassdoor sectors, which is based on a broader sample of public and private firms. TableA.2in the appendix defines all variables used in this analysis.

Figure IA4shows that certain industries, such as professional services and manufacturing, are overrepresented in our sample, whereas others (e.g., health and education, retail and trade) are underrepresented compared with the U.S. Census. In Section2.1, we present robustness analyses in which we drop the largest overrepresented sectors addressing the potential concern that the differences in industry representation found in our sample biases our results.Figure IA5compares the distribution of respondents by state between the Census and our sample and shows similar patterns.

1.3 Quarterly Workforce Indicators

We measure the percentage of female high-skilled employees at the industry level in our baseline analysis using the Quarterly Workforce Indicators (QWI) from the Census Bureau. These data are the typical source for measuring labor market flows across space and time since they draw on data from state agencies for distributing and accounting for unemployment insurance.2Specifically, we compute the percentage of female workers with a college degree or further advanced education as a fraction of the total number of employees with a college degree or further advanced education by four-digit NAICS industry group (%female high-skill). We focus on college-educated workers, consistent with the fact that skilled labor is a significant determinant of firm value (Merz and Yashiv 2007).

For the average industry in our sample, women account for 41%of all college-educated workers (Table 2). There is, however, significant variation in the distribution of high-skilled female employees across industries. Women account for less than 25%of all college-educated workers in some industries, such as in mining and rail transportation, and compose nearly 90%in child care services. These differences in gender distribution are consistent with the findings of gender segregation in the gender pay gap literature. Cultural norms, the need to balance work and household responsibilities, and differences in preferences have been shown to be determinants of this segregation. SeeBlau and Kahn (2017)for a more detailed discussion.

1.4 Paid weeks of maternity leave

As an alternative measure to maternity benefit ratings, we collect data on weeks of paid maternity leave offered by firms from Fairygodboss.com. Fairygodboss is a crowdsourcing service that aggregates reviews on firm characteristics of particular interest to female employees. Furthermore, the website does not pay reviewers for their reviews, mitigating incentives to misrepresent the quality of reviewed characteristics. We match firms on Fairygodboss.com to Glassdoor and observe data on weeks of paid leave for 38%of firms in our baseline sample of publicly listed firms. The minimum and maximum paid maternity leave are 0 and 52 weeks, respectively, with an interquartile range of 6 to 12 weeks. The average and median weeks of paid maternity leave are 9.5 and 8, respectively.

Firms in our sample with paid weeks of maternity leave data from Fairygodboss tend to be larger; average total assets for those firms is $288.9 billion, compared to $46.7 billion for firms in our sample without paid leave data from Fairygodboss. In addition, firms with paid leave data from Fairygodboss tend to be more profitable, with profit margins of 10.8%compared to 4.0%for other firms in our sample. They also have higher Tobin’s q, 3.4 compared to 2.7 for other firms in our sample. However, leverage ratios tend to be lower for firms with maternity leave data, at 22.4%compared to 25.0%for firms without maternity leave data.

1.5 Firm-level gender composition

We hand-collect data on the percentage of female employees in the firm. This information is available for public firms if they disclose a diversity and inclusion report or a diversity and inclusion section within a corporate social responsibility report.3Disclosures are voluntary, but almost one-third of the public firms in our sample (343 unique firms) opted to disclose their gender composition. For firms that disclose their gender composition, the average female worker share is 41.8%⁠, and the median is 38%⁠. According to the Census, women compose 48%of the U.S. labor force. The lower mean female percentage at firms in our sample suggests that disclosure is observed not just for firms promoting their diverse workforce. In fact, 25%of firms disclose female shares of 30%or lower.

Firms that disclose data on female worker shares tend to be larger; average total assets for disclosure firms total $313.1 billion, compared to $87.0 billion for nondisclosure firms. However, the firms otherwise appear similar, with disclosing firms having an average leverage of 21.3%and Tobin’s q of 3.2, similar to nondisclosure firms with an average leverage of 25.5%and Tobin’s q of 2.1.
article.2. Baseline Results:
2.1 Industry female labor supply, and maternity benefit ratings

Our economic intuition builds on the fact that firms operating in industries with a lower supply of high-skilled female employees will, on average, find it difficult to attract and retain female talent. Moreover, firms specifically value gender diversity among high-skilled workers. This argument is consistent with recent legislation requiring women to sit on firm boards, concerns expressed that women do not occupy higher positions in firms’ hierarchies, and concerns regarding the gender pay gap, which is at least partially explained by a lower percentage of women in high-skill and high-wage jobs (Blau and Kahn 2017). We hypothesize that firms offer relatively more generous maternity benefits to achieve greater gender diversity when the supply of female talent is low. We do not take a stance on the source behind the low supply. Regardless of the source, our argument is that individual companies may use benefit packages to achieve more gender diversity in sectors in which a low supply of college-educated women makes diversity less likely to occur organically. We, thus, explore whether maternity benefits are provided as a means to attract and retain talented female employees in industries in which female employees are traditionally underrepresented.

Maternity benefits can signal a more female-friendly culture, thereby making the firm more attractive to female employees. Consistent with the argument,Bertrand, Goldin, and Katz (2010)show, in a sample of MBAs, that mothers actively choose family-friendly jobs. Similarly,Herr and Wolfram (2012)show that a non-family-friendly work environment can “push” women out of the labor force at motherhood.4Maternity leave benefits can be a more effective substitute to paying higher cash compensation, since spending time with a newborn child, allowed by generous maternity benefits, is arguably more valuable than higher cash compensation.Bana, Bedard, and Rossin-Slater (2020)andBailey et al. (2019)find that maternity benefits increase the likelihood new mothers return to their previous employer, an effect attributed to greater worker morale and firm loyalty. Also, making progress toward diversity targets by providing higher-quality maternity benefits is likely to be viewed as a fairer practice compared to offering gender-specific wages.

We measure the relative supply of high-skilled female candidates within the firm’s (four-digit NAICS) industry as the share of college-educated workers in the industry who are female.5We present the results inTable 3. In column 1, we find a negative correlation between the percentage of high-skilled female employees in a given industry and individuals’ maternity leave benefit ratings. Our results are economically important: a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female college-educated employees in a given industry decreases expected maternity benefit ratings by 0.09, which reflects 0.07 of the standard deviation in the maternity benefit rating variable.

In our specification, we control for year fixed effects to absorb macroeconomic shocks. We also account for differences in ratings by employee characteristics. Specifically, we control for individuals’ gender, age, and education and for whether the employee is evaluating a current or previous job. On average, maternity benefit ratings are higher for current employers, potentially reflecting the fact that job separations are the result of negative employment experiences. They are also higher for employees with a college degree, consistent with the fact that firms with a more educated workforce may offer better compensation packages overall. We further include firm-level controls: firm size (ln(Assets)), profitability (Profit Margin), leverage (Debt/Assets), and investment opportunities (q). Firm size is positively associated with maternity ratings, possibly reflecting the fact that larger firms may be able to better manage extended maternity leave taken by employees. Firms with lower leverage tend to offer higher-quality maternity benefits, possibly reflecting financial constraints that limit the ability of some firms to provide generous benefits. Firms with higher investment opportunities offer better quality maternity benefits, possibly because their employees tend to be more high skilled and may receive greater overall benefits due to higher competition for talent.6

In column 2, we additionally control for state fixed effects, thereby comparing maternity benefit ratings within states across industries, and find very similar results.7In column 3, we further control for individual wages (log-transformed) to address the concern that high-wage industries may provide greater nonwage benefits as well as be male dominated. Our sample size drops because base pay is not reported for all individuals.8If our results are indeed driven by an omitted variable associated with total compensation, then the%female high-skillcoefficient should lose significance once wages are accounted for in our regression. Although positive, the relation between base pay and maternity benefits is not statistically significant. Most importantly, the negative relationship with the percentage of female high-skilled employees remains strongly statistically and economically significant.9

2.2 Robustness tests

InTable 4, we repeat column 3,Table 3, and show that our results are robust to additional controls, dropping specific subsamples, weighting, and imputing zero ratings. We begin by controlling for the gender of top executives in column 1. Industries with skewed gender distributions in high-skill workers may also exhibit skewed gender differences in executives.Tate and Yang (2015)show that firms with women in leadership positions have more female-friendly cultures, which may substitute for the provision of better quality maternity benefits. To this end, we define the share of executives that are female and find our results are robust.

Our findings rely on the argument that firms offer nonwage benefits to attract female talent instead of paying higher wages. To further confirm this intuition, in column 2,Table 4, we show that our results are robust to controlling for differences across firms in the pay of women relative to men,Gender pay gap. If relatively higher pay for women can effectively achieve a gender balanced workforce in the firm, then adding this control may cause the relationship between the distribution of female talent and maternity benefits to lose significance. Instead, we continue to find a significant coefficient for%female high-skillafter controlling for the firm-level average pay ratio between women and men, among the employees reporting their pay to Glassdoor. These results also show that the costs of providing maternity benefits do not appear to be offset by differential wages by gender. This result contrasts withGruber (1994), who finds that firms shift costs associated with greater health insurance mandates to the wages of those employees most likely to consume these benefits. The use of additive benefits is consistent with our interpretation of firms using paid maternity leave to attract and retain more women.

Moreover, we might be concerned that some individuals’ reviews may be uninformed or biased. In particular, men may be less aware of these benefits. In column 3 ofTable 4, we drop male respondents from our sample and continue to find a significantly negative relationship between female high-skill share and maternity benefits. Rating tampering presents another potential concern. Firms may try to influence their current employees to give positive reviews on Glassdoor. However, this should be less of a concern for individuals reviewing their past employer. Therefore, in column 4, we drop respondents reviewing their current employer, and find that our results continue to hold.

We also address concerns that a few overrepresented industries or firms drive our results. In column 5, we drop respondents in the technology and finance industries, the two largest industries in our sample, and continue to find a negative and significant relationship between female high-skill share and maternity benefits. In column 6, we implement a weighted ordinary least squares (OLS) regression in which weights are one divided by the total number of respondents in a given firm. If our findings are driven by a few large firms, then this inverse weighting scheme, which down-weights observations in larger firms, should result in a loss in significance in our coefficient of interest. Instead, we find that the negative relationship between female high-skill share and maternity benefits persists with similar magnitude.

Lastly, in column 7, we show that our results are robust to setting the rating to zero for firms without maternity leave benefits. We incorporate data from an alternative database from Glassdoor, which asks users if benefits are available at their employer. Using these data, we impute zero maternity ratings and add these observations to our baseline sample. Specifically, we assign a maternity benefit rating of zero to individuals who work for firms without a maternity benefit rating and who claim that their firm does not have a maternity rating. Under this imputation scheme, we continue to find a negative and significant coefficient for%female high-skill.

2.3 Female-friendly versus gender-neutral benefit ratings

Our economic argument that nonwage benefits can be used by firms to target underrepresented employees to the extent they are disproportionately valuable to them extends beyond maternity benefits. To this end, we expand our analysis to include all benefits rated in Glassdoor, resulting in a much larger sample compared with our baseline analysis. As inTable 3, this sample includes all individuals who provided a review of an employer’s benefit on Glassdoor, during the July 2014 through April 2019 period, for whom we observe nonmissing individual characteristics and who rate a firm that can be matched to Compustat. However, we no longer restrict the sample to only those respondents reviewing maternity benefits. We assign each benefit as being either female friendly (i.e., disproportionately valued by women) or gender neutral.10

We classify benefits to be female friendly when they either support working mothers directly or relate to health insurance, which is motivated by the fact that women use medical services more often than men and incur higher medical expenses than men (e.g.,Fuchs (1974);Andersen, Lion, and Anderson (1976);Cleary, Mechanic, and Greenley (1982);Verbrugge (1982)).11This is consistent with sociological theories suggesting that health perceptions and decisions regarding medical care can differ by gender. For example,Feldman, Kralewski, and Dowd (1989)show that female workers are more likely than male workers to use preventative care.Wilensky and Cafferata (1983)conclude that greater medical use by women is associated with lower perceived health status and a greater responsiveness to chronic conditions.Sindelar (1982)argues that men are more likely to use home medical care instead of professional medical care as a cheaper substitute.Leong and Zachar (1999),Mackenzie, Gekoski, and Knox (2006), andNam et al. (2010)show that women are more likely to utilize mental health services than men.Cleary, Mechanic, and Greenley (1982)argues that sex differences in chronic health conditions and childcare expenses are the most important factors that can explain higher use of medical care by women.12Ultimately, our classification is guided by the assumption that a given benefit is disproportionately valuable to women relative to men and does not require that a given benefit should have zero value for men.

InTable 5, panel A, we repeat our baseline specification but now looking at the relation between%female high-skilland female-friendly benefits in columns 1–3. Since this analysis involves multiple benefits, we control for benefit fixed effects in all our specifications. We confirm our hypothesis that firms in industries with lower representation of high-skilled female workers offer more generous female-friendly benefits. These results are economically important: a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female high-skilled employees in an industry is associated with a decrease in female-friendly benefit ratings by 0.10 rating points, reflecting 0.08 of the standard deviation in female-friendly benefit ratings.13

One concern with our results so far is that differences across industries, unrelated to achieving gender diversity, could correlate with both the supply of female talent and firms’ compensation policies. To the extent that such differences drive our results, we should observe similar outcomes when we consider gender-neutral benefits. In panel A, columns 4–6, we conduct a placebo analysis by looking at the relation between%female high-skilland gender-neutral benefits. If industries that have low female talent representation tend to offer better compensation packages overall, then we should observe a statistically significant relationship when considering these placebo benefits. In contrast to this alternative interpretation, we find no statistical relationship between gender-neutral benefits and the pool of high-skill female labor supply in the industry.

By using this broader sample of female-friendly and gender-neutral benefits, we are able to include more detailed controls that allow us to refute additional alternative interpretations of our findings. InTable 5, panel B, we present a pooled regression specification, where we focus on the interaction coefficient between%female high-skillandFemale-friendly, whereFemale-friendlyis an indicator variable equal to one if a review pertains to a female-friendly benefit, and zero otherwise. In columns 1 and 2, we repeat the specifications in panel A, but within this pooled framework. Consistent with our earlier findings, we show that relative to gender-neutral benefits, a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female high-skill employees in a given industry decreases expected benefit rating by an additional 0.07 rating points for female-friendly benefits, which reflects 0.05 of the standard deviation in the benefits rating variable (column 2). In column 3, we control for firm-year fixed effects, in addition to the state and benefit fixed effects in the previous specification, thereby absorbing any time-varying firm-level characteristics that could affect benefit quality. In column 4, we further saturate our specification with interacted firm times year times user-job fixed effects, thereby comparing benefit ratings within the same user (who evaluates a given job) in a given firm and year.

These documented differences between female-friendly and gender-neutral benefits, conditional on a given user evaluating a specific job in a given firm and year, support a causal interpretation of our findings. First, they rule out the possibility that a time-varying firm-level omitted variable, such as requiring longer hours from employees and offering better overall compensation packages, drive our results. Second, they address concerns related to the fact that ratings may reflect individuals’ overall attitudes toward their company rather than the quality of the benefits offered, or that employees in certain industries might be valuing benefits more, and especially if those industries are also those characterized by a low supply of female talent. Third, they confirm that our results are not driven by composition effects or selection of individuals into rating their firms’ benefits. Fourth, they indicate that our findings cannot be driven by firms manipulating ratings, as we document these effects within a given firm-user-job.

Finally, these analyses also provide useful external validity to our baseline results and show our economic intuition is not limited to maternity benefits. Nevertheless, we focus our empirical analysis on maternity benefit provision due to the importance of understanding the implications of maternity leave on firm outcomes and our ability to externally validate the quality of maternity leave policies using an alternative data source, as shown in Section3.
article.3. External Validity and Mechanisms:
In this section, we present additional analyses that provide external validity to our findings. First, we use an alternative measure of maternity leave benefit generosity. Second, we measure the female high-skill labor supply at the state rather than the industry level. Both tests produce similar findings. Finally, we discuss the mechanisms that speak to the effectiveness of these policies toward increasing gender diversity.

3.1 Ratings versus actual benefits

In our baseline analysis, we use the rating of a given benefit and interpret the rating as a proxy for benefit quality. We argue that the overall quality of maternity benefits is more informative than narrower measures, such as weeks of paid leave, as it accounts for both quantitative features, such as the number of paid weeks, and qualitative features, such as concerns about whether taking allowed maternity leave may have negative ramifications on one’s future career outcomes (Zagorsky 2017). However, our measure could be potentially biased if individual perceptions matter and comove with the female labor supply. We address this concern to an extent inTable 5, panel B, as our results hold even within a given user-job-firm-year. In this section, we further address this concern by replicating our earlier results using a measure of maternity benefits that is independent from user judgment, weeks of paid leave.

Specifically, we use information on the number of weeks of paid maternity leave offered by firms and repeat our baseline specification. The duration of paid maternity leave—just one aspect of overall maternity benefits—is both an important component and an easy one to measure. A univariate regression of weeks of paid maternity leave on the firm’s median maternity rating gives a positive and statistically significant coefficient at the 1%level. We observe a snapshot of Fairygodboss.com data, available at the time of data collection, and thus are limited to a cross-sectional analysis at the firm level.

InTable 6, column 1, we show a negative correlation between the percentage of high-skilled female workers in the industry and the number of weeks of paid maternity leave, after controlling for the same firm-level controls as in our baseline analysis (firm size, profitability, leverage, and Q). The results are statistically weaker relative to our baseline analysis, likely because of the smaller sample size and because policies may be offered, but not used, if the culture of the firm does not encourage it. However, they are economically important: a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of female high-skilled employees in an industry is associated with a decrease in paid weeks of maternity leave by approximately 10%⁠. In columns 2 and 3, we additionally control for the firm’s reviewer characteristics (median employee age, share of female workers, share of college-educated workers) as well as region fixed effects, respectively, and find similar results. In column 4, our results are robust to controlling for median base pay in the firm.

3.2 Female labor supply by geography

Our intuition that firms use maternity benefits to attract female talent in labor markets in which it is relatively scarce also can be tested by defining labor markets based on geographies. Firms operating in states with a limited supply of female talent may similarly choose to offer better maternity benefits to compete for the smaller pool of talented women. We use the location of the respondents’ employment on Glassdoor, effectively assuming that the geographic distribution of respondents’ employment reflects the firm’s physical presence.

3.2.1 Instrumental variable analysis

We next augment our state-level analysis with an alternative identification strategy that exploits plausibly exogenous variation in World War II mobilization rates (WWII mobilization rate), specifically the percentage of a state’s prewar population that fought in that conflict, as an instrument for%female high-skill (state). We estimate WWII mobilization rates as the fraction of a state’s population that served in WWII. The original data are published in the Selective Service System monograms (Selective Service System 1956). While all eligible males between the ages of 18 and 44 were registered for selective service, there was substantial heterogeneity in actual draft rates across states. According toAcemoglu, Autor, and Lyle (2004), there were three significant drivers of WWII mobilization rates: farm employment, schooling, and ethnicity.

We predict thatWWII Mobilization Ratesare negatively associated with current female college attainment because, when more men in a state left to serve in the war efforts, women were more likely to join the labor force. In particular, women in these states were more likely to work in low-skill jobs, as they were previously out of the labor force and were less likely to have adequate training for high-skill jobs. These geographical differences in low-skilled female labor force participation during WWII persisted over time as the next generation of women in these communities followed their role models into similar jobs. A number of studies provide support for this argument.Goldin and Olivetti (2013)show that states with greater mobilization of men in WWII experienced higher postwar female labor force participation.Acemoglu, Autor, and Lyle (2004)find similar results, but also show that postwar female labor was a substitute for non-college-educated men, suggesting that new female entrants in the work force were unlikely to hold a college degree. As work opportunities for non-college-educated females increased, so did the opportunity costs of college education.15Similarly,Jaworski (2014)also shows that WWII mobilization resulted in lower postwar education attainment for women. Given that inter-generational college attainment is highly correlated (Black and Devereux 2011), mobilization during WWII should reduce female college attainment today.

For this analysis, we collapse our sample to the state level, weighting observations by the number of Glassdoor respondents per state. Specifically, we averageMaternity benefit rating,%female high-skill (state), andGDP per capitawithin each state. We do this because our instrument exhibits cross-sectional variation only at the state level.

Column 1 ofTable 8displays the state-level OLS regression of maternity benefit quality on the female high-skilled share, controlling for demographic characteristics in 1940 and contemporaneous state-level characteristics. We confirm a negative and significant association. Columns 2 and 3 display the first and second stage of our IV regressions, respectively. As expected, column 2 shows that WWII mobilization rates are negatively associated with current state-level female high-skill composition. Our instrument is significant at the 1%level with an F-statistic of 10.71. In the second stage regression, in column 3, the instrumented coefficient for college-educated female percentage is negative and significant. The magnitude of the effect increases in the IV specification, relative to the OLS estimate in column 1, suggesting that endogeneity is biasing the OLS effect downward. This result could be consistent with the fact that areas with lower female college workforce participation are areas with social norms discouraging working women (Bertrand, Kamenica, and Pan 2015). Firms operating in areas with such social norms may be less likely to offer generous maternity benefits.

As with all instrumental variables analysis, interpretation of the result depends on the ability to justify the exclusion restriction. We control for state demographic characteristics in 1940, just before the entrance of the United States into WWII. Specifically, we control for states’ white and black share, male share, and age distribution. Age distribution is controlled for using age bins that track the fraction of each state’s 1940s population belonging in each 5-year bin (i.e., 0–4, 5–9, 10–14, ... , 70–74, 75+). We additionally control for other state-level factors that might be correlated with both mobilization rates and the quality of current maternity benefits: gross domestic product (GDP) per capita and political orientation, proxied for by theCook Partisan Lean Index.

Moreover, we consider each of the drivers of state-level variation in WWII mobilization rates found inAcemoglu, Autor, and Lyle (2004)and do our best to rule out any direct relationship between these drivers of WWII mobilization variation and the current provision of maternity benefits by firms. Variation in the characteristics of the local population as we well as variation in how draft boards interpreted the guidance led to differences in WWII mobilization rates across states.

Given the critical importance of maintaining the food supply, local draft boards took into account agricultural employment. Consistent with this argument,Acemoglu, Autor, and Lyle (2004)show that state farming household shares pre-WWII are significantly negatively correlated with WWII mobilization rates. In terms of our analysis, a concern could be that predominantly rural states might be more inclined to adhere to more traditional family structures with more conservative views toward women in the workforce. To the extent that these more conservative views on women in the workforce are passed down across generations, this could affect maternity benefit provision generosity by local firms. We attempt to control for any “direct” impact of this WWII mobilization driver on average state-level maternity benefits 70 years later by adding a new control that captures local variation in support of working mothers. The variable, from the General Social Survey (GSS),Working mother not harmful to childrencaptures the percentage of respondents in a given region that agree with the statement “a working mother can establish just as warm and secure a relationship with her children as a mother who does not work.” Although these data are regional, they directly measure the geographical variation in the acceptance of working mothers. Importantly, inInternet Appendix A Table IA7, columns 1 and 2, we show the IV results are robust after controlling forWorking mother not harmful to children.

Second,Acemoglu, Autor, and Lyle (2004)find that the average schooling among men in 1940 positively predicts mobilization rates. One concern is that if higher rates of college education among men in 1940 was driven by differential ease of access to college locally, then this could affect the provision of maternity benefits 70 years later given more educated workers typically receive better compensation packages. Using data from the Department of Education, we create a new variableUniversity count, which measures the number of Universities in a given state as of 2020. Columns 3 and 4 show the IV results are robust to includingUniversity count.

Third,Acemoglu, Autor, and Lyle (2004)argue that the German immigrant share of a state’s male population could also predict mobilization rates, as draft boards were concerned about soldiers’ motivation to fight against their country of origin. Indeed, they show that German immigrant shares pre-WWII are significantly negatively associated with mobilization rates. Furthermore, German immigrants are more likely to be Mainline Protestants, who are less likely to be devoutly religious and less likely to hold conservative views when compared to other Christian denominations (Pew Research Center 2014). Less conservative views may affect attitudes toward women in the workforce today. This variation in religiosity could thus affect our IV analysis to the extent that religious practices are passed down across generations and stricter religious adherence may affect maternity benefits provision by firms. To control for this possible direct channel, we measure local religiosity using survey responses from the GSS. The variableAttends service weeklyis measured as fraction of individuals who attend religious service at least once of week, as of year 2018. Columns 5 and 6 show the IV analysis is robust to controlling forAttends service weekly.

3.3 Mechanisms

Our findings are consistent with the idea that firms provide generous maternity benefits to achieve greater gender diversity when female talent is scarce. Next, we show that providing better quality maternity benefits in industries with a low share of female high-skilled employees is indeed associated with a more balanced gender distribution, suggesting that this strategy is effective. To do so, we hand-collect data on the percentage of female employees in the firm, when available for public firms, as described in Section1.

We present the results inTable 9. Our coefficient of interest isMedian maternity benefit rating×Low%female high-skill, whereMedian maternity benefit ratingis the firm median maternity benefit rating andLow%female high-skillis an indicator equal to one if the firm’s industry has below median%female high-skill, and zero otherwise. We find that providing better quality maternity benefits, when female talent in the industry is scarce, is associated with a higher percentage of female employees in the firm. Although female talent scarcity in the industry is on average associated with lower firm-level female representation, this effect is mitigated for firms with higher-quality maternity benefits. These results are robust to controlling for firm-level controls, firms’ median reviewer characteristics, and region and sector fixed effects. In the most robust specification (column 5), a one-point increase in maternity rating is associated with a 2.6 percentage point increase in female employment for firms in industries with low female talent. Despite the limited sample size, these results suggest that offering more generous maternity benefits can result in a more gender-balanced workforce to the extent that it helps firms to hire or retain female talent.

Although, arguably, it is challenging to separate between the two mechanisms given the intrinsic relationship between retention and hiring, we provide suggestive evidence that collectively supports both channels. First, we find evidence that potential employees are aware of maternity benefit provision prior to hiring. To this end, we hand-collect data from the employment website Indeed.com as to whether the publicly listed firms in our sample advertise maternity leave benefits in their online job posting ads. Assuming that job seekers will have limited attention, variation in job descriptions can be explained by firms emphasizing the most relevant details to attract the best candidates for their needs.Internet Appendix A Table IA8shows a positive correlation between the median maternity benefit rating in a given firm and whether the firm mentions maternity leave benefits in their ads. We further show that this relationship is stronger for firms operating in industries with a lower supply of female talent. These results suggest that maternity benefits play a role in hiring female employees at the firm.

Second, inInternet Appendix A Table IA9, we repeat our baseline analysis inTable 3, but run a horserace by interacting%female high-skillwith industry female and male turnover, respectively. We show that the positive association between maternity benefit ratings and scarcity of female talent at the industry is more pronounced in industries with higher female turnover, namely industries in which the retention of female employees should most matter. However, we find no effect when, in the same specification, we condition instead on higher male turnover industries.

Taken together, these findings suggest that the motive of providing better quality maternity benefits is consistent with our sorting hypothesis rather than an alternative hypothesis where firms provide more generous maternity benefits when they have few female employees and hence the benefit will be less costly to provide. Indeed, we found that firms that offer more generous maternity benefits in fact tend to have more female employees, compared to their peers. Moreover, given the low frequency with which women have children, the cost of providing maternity benefits does not seem to be prohibitively high for firms. Among firms with paid maternity leave in our sample, the average length of coverage is 11.35 weeks (22%of a year). In 2019, the average U.S. wage was $41,135. As such, the typical maternity leave wage expense will cost the firm $8,979. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), there were 58.3 births per 1000 women aged 15-44.16Given 27.8%of employees in the United States (using QWI) are women between these ages and the median firm in our sample has 23,700 employees, this translates to an aggregate cost of $3.4M per year or $143 per employee. This likely overestimates the wage costs as women giving birth are likely in the earlier stages of their career. At the same time, these calculations do not account for disruptions that result from women taking leave.
article.4. Do Generous, Voluntarily Offered Mate:
In this section, we explore value implications of voluntarily offered maternity benefits using the quasi-exogenous passage of state laws mandating paid family leave. Five states (New York, Washington, Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Oregon) and Washington DC passed legislation that mandated family leave benefits for all local employees during our sample years. In addition, California and New Jersey expanded their existing paid family leave policies.17Workers covered under these plans could receive payments, funded by a new tax, following the birth or adoption of a child, based on past wages. When a state provides paid maternity leave (albeit with coverage limits), existing voluntary plans by firms becomes less effective at promoting gender diversity since the relative gap between the benefits available from these firms and their peers is reduced. We provide detailed descriptions of state paid family leave legislation inInternet Appendix B.18

We use the quasi-exogenous passage of these laws to understand the value implications of generous voluntary maternity benefits that are intended to promote gender diversity. In Table 10 , we estimate an event study analysis whereby we compare the stock price reaction of treated firms (firms headquartered in states that passed paid family leave laws) to that of control firms (firms headquartered in states that did not pass such laws). 19 For each firm, we measure cumulative 3-day abnormal returns over a [ − 1 , + 1 ] window around the dates the legislation was passed. We use a capital asset pricing model (CAPM) to infer expected returns, where the CAPM beta is estimated using a 252-day window prior to the event. Our sample includes all firms with employer maternity benefit reviews on Glassdoor from July 2014 through April 2019. The sample is restricted to firms that can be matched to Compustat and CRSP. Our coefficient of interest is the interaction coefficient,β1⁠, which estimates the differential stock price reaction to a state mandate for firms voluntarily offering generous maternity benefits, while operating in a labor market in which female talent is scarce.

If firms already offering high maternity benefits in industries with relatively fewer talented women were benefiting from this strategy, then a state leave mandate should reduce the relative effectiveness of this strategy by shrinking the gap in maternity benefit policies between these ex ante high-benefit firms and their peers. Consistent with this intuition, we find a negative and significant effect onLaw×high maternity×Low%female high-skill. On average, in the 3 days around the passage of state laws, firms headquartered in a treated state with high ex ante maternity ratings (relative to firms in the same industry) and drawing from a labor market with a low supply of high-skilled women, experience an abnormal return of−1.0 to−1.4%⁠, relative to control firms. In column 2, we include firm-level controls forln(Assets),Profit margin,Debt/Assets, andq, as in our baseline analysis. In column 3, we additionally control for industry-state fixed effects to capture time invariant differences in industries across states, while in column 4 we instead add firm fixed effects to control for any time-invariant firm characteristics (which subsumes industry-state fixed effects).20Finally, in column 5 we infer expected returns using a four-factor model (Carhart 1997) and our results continue to hold.

In columns 6–10, we repeat the same specifications as in columns 1–5, except we focus on the impact of paid family leave laws on firms with high maternity benefits relative to firms in their state. Specifically, we reestimate Equation (1), but instead replaceLow%female high skill (ind)withLow%female high skill (state), which takes the value one (zero) if a firm is headquartered in a state with an above (below) median female high-skill share pretreatment, where female high-skill share is calculated as the female share of college educated workers in the firm’s headquarter state. We now also redefineHigh maternityto take the value one (zero) if a firm’s average pretreatment maternity benefit rating is above (below) the state median. Our coefficient of interest,Law×high maternity leave×Low%female high-skill (state), remains negative and statistically significant across specifications. On average, in the 3 days around the passage of state laws mandating paid family leave benefits, high maternity firms in their state that draw from a labor market with a low supply of high-skilled women experience an abnormal return of−1.2 to−2.0%⁠, relative to control firms. It is worth noting when interpreting the magnitudes that we do not expect these laws to equalize maternity benefits across firms in a given state (or industry), as the benefits provided through the legislation are limited in terms of weeks of leave and caps on payments. Instead, the magnitudes of the effect represent a partial closing of the wedge between high- and low-maternity benefit firms.

We focus on the impact of the law on the subset of firms with ex ante generous maternity benefits in low female share states/industries as we are interested in the value implications of these generous voluntarily provided maternity plans. In essence, we are measuring an indirect effect of these laws by looking at how they affect the effectiveness of existing maternity plans by changing the offerings at peer firms in the same labor market. However, our specification also allows us to make interesting conclusions about the direct effects of mandatory state-funded maternity benefits on firm value. We will discuss these next.

Mandatory paid family leave laws will directly affect firm value through multiple channels. These laws should increase the ability of firms that previously did not have maternity benefits to achieve gender diversity and this should be most valuable for firms operating in industries with low shares of high-skill women.21The positive coefficient forLaw×Low%female high-skill (ind)is consistent with this argument.22These laws have positive average effects on firms that operate in industries in which female talent is scarce, after controlling for the negative effect of the law among firms that had ex ante generous benefits and that operate in labor markets in which female talent is scarce. Offering maternity leave will be advantageous to firms to the extent that doing so helps them achieve beneficial gender diversity when it is relatively more challenging to do so, namely when few talented women are in a given labor market, all else equal.

Another channel by which paid family leave laws can directly affect firm value is by effectively subsidizing, at least partially, existing maternity leave programs at firms. The law creates a tax on wages that creates a pool of funding from which women who take maternity leave can receive compensation. To the extent that these laws affect firm value through this subsidization channel, then we should observe a positive coefficient forLaw×High maternity. This coefficient picks up the impact of the law on firms that had ex ante high maternity benefits, after controlling for the negative effect of the law among firms that had ex ante generous benefits and that operate in labor markets in which female talent is scarce. While we find no significant effect in the industry specifications (columns 1–5), we find a positive and significant coefficient forLaw×High maternityacross all five state specifications (columns 6–10). These laws effectively subsidize, at least partially, existing maternity leave programs at firms, with the gains going disproportionately to firms with ex ante more generous maternity plans.
article.5. Conclusion:
Using unique, crowdsourced data on nonwage benefits, as well as wages and other employee characteristics, we propose employee sorting as an explanation that can drive the use of some benefits. To test this channel, we focus on maternity benefits, a type of benefit particularly valued by women. Our evidence suggests that the provision of better maternity benefits appears to be used by firms to achieve greater gender diversity. We find that firms in labor markets with a lower supply of high-skilled women are more likely to offer more generous maternity benefits. Disproportionately valued by women, maternity benefits may signal a female-friendly corporate culture, while having the added benefit of being viewed as a fairer practice to increase diversity relative to providing differential cash compensation by gender.

We also document that firms voluntarily offering generous maternity benefits and operating in labor markets in which female talent is scarce experience a drop in stock prices around legislation events mandating paid family leave. These government programs decrease the relative generosity of plans that were voluntarily offered by firms prior to the legislation, as women now can receive a minimum set of maternity benefits from all firms. These results are consistent with arguments by investors, policy makers, and CEOs that (gender) diversity is associated with positive firm outcomes.

Our findings that firms offer nonwage benefits to attract key talent could be one driving force of the increasing importance of nonmonetary types of compensation for firms over time given the increasing demand for high-skilled workers in the production process over the same period and the increased competition among firms to attract the right talent (Zingales 2000). Our results are also related to female labor force participation. This is an acute concern as we exit the global COVID-19 pandemic. Throughout the pandemic, women have dropped out of the labor force at a higher rate compared to men (Alon et al. 2020;Barber et al. 2021). It is thus pertinent, now more than ever, to understand the drivers of attracting female talent to the labor force, as this knowledge can greatly benefit firms and has far-reaching societal implications.

The difference is possibly driven by the fact that private firms are smaller in size and, as shown in Brown and Medoff (1989) , smaller firms pay lower wages. Despite less geographical—compared to industry—variation, significant variation remains in the distribution of high-skilled women across states, with an average of 47 % in our sample of public and private firms. In Vermont and Maine, female workers represent 52.9 % and 53.1 % of all college-educated workers, respectively. On the lower end, female workers in Utah and Wyoming represent only 38.1 % and 43.2 % of all college-educated workers, respectively.

18 Our analysis relates to a recent literature exploring the implications of state mandatory maternity benefit laws on female labor force participation and long-run firm characteristics. Two recent papers by Bailey et al. (2019) and Bana, Bedard, and Rossin-Slater (2020) , using administrative data and regression discontinuity designs, find no significant impact on overall female employment following the Californian family leave law. However, both papers find that, conditional on being employed, new mothers are more likely to return to their prebirth employer following a mandate. These papers, thus, suggest that paid family leave changed the allocation of female talent, even in the absence of an increase in total female employment. From a firm’s perspective, the ability to better retain young mothers is valuable. Consistent with this argument, Bennett et al. (2021) find an ex post positive impact on firm productivity, driven by reduced turnover and higher female leadership following the adoption of state-level paid family leave laws.
